title: NPC Speech Quirks
description: A distinctive verbal habit, pattern, or tic that makes an NPC immediately recognizable in play — usable in any genre.

entries.result: Answers questions with a question first. Always.
Refers to themselves in the third person when discussing their professional role.
Ends statements with a confirming phrase ('You understand?', 'Are we clear?', 'Right?') that suggests chronic doubt about whether they're being heard.
Quotes proverbs frequently — sometimes correctly applied, sometimes not quite.
Uses extremely precise language and corrects themselves when they use a word that isn't quite right.
Trails off mid-sentence when they think the listener should already know the ending.
Narrates their actions while they do them. 'I'm setting this down now. I'm turning to face you.'
Starts almost every response with a brief, considered silence before speaking.
Uses understatement so consistently that sincerity reads as sarcasm until you know them.
Repeats the last few words of any statement they want to emphasize.
Speaks in an oddly formal register that shifts to complete informality when they're angry or tired.
Uses a specific filler word or phrase constantly without seeming to notice it ('essentially,' 'as it were,' 'in point of fact').
Refers to everyone by their profession or role rather than their name, even people they know well.
Gives exactly the literal answer to every question, no more and no less, which can make them seem evasive.
Tells very long stories to answer simple questions. The stories are always relevant, eventually.
Speaks quickly and then stops completely, waiting for a response before continuing.
Hedges every claim with qualifiers. 'It's possible,' 'I could be wrong,' 'This is only my impression.'
Addresses abstract concepts as if they are people. 'Ask caution what it thinks. Caution says no.'
Speaks exclusively in short, direct sentences. Never subordinate clauses. Always declarative.
Apologizes before saying anything that might be inconvenient, regardless of whether it is.
Frames every opinion as a question. 'Isn't it generally agreed that…?' 'Wouldn't most people say…?'
Describes their emotional state with physical sensations. 'I notice my chest tightening.' 'Something relaxes in my shoulders.'
Never uses contractions. This gives their speech a slightly formal, slightly mechanical quality.
Uses hyperbole for everything, including minor matters. 'I've been waiting for an eternity.' 'This room is the size of a kingdom.'
Asks for clarification before answering any ambiguous question. Any. Ambiguous. Question.
Tells you the number of something when the precise number wasn't important. 'I have seven concerns.' 'That was three weeks and four days ago.'
Uses metaphors from one specific domain (navigation, cooking, agriculture) regardless of context.
Summarizes what the listener just said before responding, as though taking minutes.
Speaks only in the present tense, even when discussing past or future events.
Starts every conversation by establishing whether this is 'a short conversation or a long one.'
Uses the word 'allegedly' with great frequency, regardless of whether allegation is in question.
Laughs at their own jokes before delivering the punchline.
Never says 'no' directly. Always offers an alternative or a reframe instead.
Speaks at a consistent and slightly too-slow pace that makes people want to finish their sentences.
Uses 'we' to mean themselves alone, without apparent awareness that this is unusual.
Prefaces bad news with an extended positive observation about the listener.
Interrupts themselves to correct themselves, often multiple times in one sentence.
Uses a specific gesture to punctuate important points — tapping the table, touching their collar, pointing upward.
Tends to whisper in crowded rooms and project in quiet ones.
Asks 'Does that make sense?' after every explanation, not out of doubt but out of habit.
Completes any phrase the listener starts, accurately, because they've heard it all before.
Speaks as though everything is being recorded for posterity, with careful attribution and sourcing.
Uses the full name of anyone they're talking about, never a shortened form or nickname.
Makes observations about the conversation itself as it's happening. 'This is where you'd usually argue with me.'
Ends arguments by listing the main points of the opposing view before their own, which makes them very hard to dismiss.
Finds something to compliment in every statement before responding to it.
Speaks faster when nervous and doesn't notice they're doing it.
Tends to pause mid-conversation to visibly think, holding up a hand as if asking for a moment.
Uses phrases from old texts or formal documents — 'hereinafter,' 'notwithstanding,' 'in the matter of.'
Refers to past events with enormous precision and present events with vagueness, giving conversations an inverted quality.
Seems to be translating from another language, even when they're not.
Never uses a person's name. Addresses everyone as 'friend,' 'colleague,' 'stranger,' or similar.
Overexplains their own reasoning, as if they've been misunderstood many times before.
Preemptively answers objections that haven't been raised yet.
Makes small sounds of acknowledgment (mmhm, I see, right) throughout conversations that are neutral but continuous.
Introduces new subjects by saying 'on an unrelated matter' even when the matters are clearly related.
States prices, times, and quantities as ranges rather than specific values. 'Two to four days.' 'Thirty to forty coins.'
Phrases requests as observations. 'It seems like the door should be closed.' 'One might expect this floor to be swept.'
Uses historical or regional examples to make points, assuming the listener is familiar with them.
Signs off every conversation with a specific phrase that functions as a verbal goodbye ritual.
Asks 'Is that what you meant?' after every response, as if checking they understood correctly.
Identifies the genre of a conversation out loud. 'This is becoming an argument.' 'We're now negotiating.'
Uses diminutives constantly — 'a bit,' 'a little,' 'a moment,' 'a small matter' — regardless of actual scale.
Refuses to use the word 'problem.' Substitutes 'situation,' 'circumstance,' 'development,' or 'challenge.'
Gives their opinion as if reading aloud from a personal policy document.
Finds and names the silver lining of every negative statement. Even inappropriate ones.
Consistently misidentifies people's emotions with great confidence. 'You seem relieved.' (You do not seem relieved.)
Uses 'we' to refer to whatever group they most recently identified with, which changes frequently.
Can only accept compliments by immediately giving a larger compliment in return.
Attributes wisdom to a deceased mentor whose name they invoke constantly.
Uses extreme precision about time. 'I was there for approximately forty minutes and perhaps eleven seconds.'
Discusses every topic as if it's the most interesting subject in the world, because to them, it is.
Prefaces any request by explaining why they don't usually ask for things.
Speaks entirely in complete, grammatically perfect sentences, which sounds slightly unnatural.
Refers to their past self as a different person. 'The version of me that made that choice was wrong.'
Drops into a regional accent or dialect when discussing their hometown or childhood.
Uses very long words when short ones would do, but without pretension — it's simply how they think.
Has a consistent and easily identifiable verbal tic that appears when they're lying.
Says 'frankly' before the only honest things in a conversation full of polite misdirection.
Introduces themselves by their role before their name, every time, regardless of context.
Gives a brief oral history of any location they're meeting in before beginning business.
Uses sentence structures from formal rhetoric — lists of three, repetition for emphasis, chiasmus.
Refers to everything they own as 'my current [item],' implying there have been many.
Changes subjects by summarizing the previous one in a single phrase, then moving on.
Answers questions about the future the same way they answer questions about the past: with confidence and specificity.
Speaks about their emotions with the clinical detachment of a physician describing someone else's symptoms.
Categorizes every statement they make — 'This is opinion,' 'This is fact,' 'This is speculation.'
Has a recurring phrase they use when they think the conversation is about to go wrong ('here it comes,' 'naturally,' 'of course').
Defaults to the passive voice when discussing their own failures. Things happened. Mistakes were made.
Uses the active voice for everything, including things where the actor is unclear. 'The city decided.' 'The situation chose.'
Pauses after questions and waits for the answer even if the question was rhetorical.
Drops the subject of sentences as if it were obvious. 'Will arrive tomorrow.' 'Seems unlikely.' 'Told me as much.'
Uses the phrase 'in my experience' before every piece of advice, whether the experience is relevant or not.
Finishes other people's sentences — accurately — which is either impressive or deeply irritating.
Never acknowledges a compliment. Responds as if it weren't said and continues with business.
Makes predictions constantly, out loud, and tracks whether they were right. 'As I expected.' 'That surprised me.'
Uses 'one' instead of 'I' or 'you,' giving their statements a gently impersonal authority.
Asks a person's name and repeats it back multiple times in the first few minutes of meeting them.
Offers unsolicited feedback on communication style during conversations. 'That phrasing was effective.' 'You lost me there.'
Speaks so softly that people frequently lean in, and then continues at exactly the same volume.
Habitually undercuts their own authority mid-statement. 'And I should say, I've been wrong before.'
Speaks in threes, naturally restructuring thoughts to come in groups of three.
Punctuates difficult admissions with a specific physical gesture, like smoothing their clothing or rubbing the back of their neck.
Gives instructions in reverse order, ending with the first step.
Uses collective nouns for groups of unrelated things. 'A complexity of problems.' 'A depth of decisions.'
Delivers every statement as if it's a reluctant concession.
Uses silence as punctuation — long pauses between thoughts that feel like periods, not hesitations.
Refers to distant events as 'recent' and recent events as 'some time ago.'
Makes declarations rather than statements. 'It is settled.' 'That is the end of it.' 'So it is.'
Always asks whether the listener is comfortable before delivering any information of consequence.
Compresses long histories into single sentences and expands simple facts into lengthy explanations.
Assigns motivations to inanimate objects. 'The door is trying to tell us something.' 'The fire is impatient.'
Apologizes for things that are not their fault and never apologies for things that are.
Uses a slightly different vocabulary depending on who they're speaking to, seamlessly, without affectation.
Treats every question as if it has been poorly formed and spends time rephrasing it before answering.
Identifies every agreement with a brief ceremony — a nod, a handshake, a specific phrase.
Frames all plans in terms of what could go wrong rather than what is intended to happen.
Consistently attributes their own ideas to 'someone once told me' and someone else's ideas to common sense.
Ends every conversation by asking if there's anything else, even in circumstances where that would be strange.
Speaks entirely in questions when they are in agreement. 'Wouldn't that be the best approach?' 'Isn't that exactly right?'
Uses placeholders when they can't think of a word — 'the thing, you know the thing' — and often doesn't fill them in.
Structures every explanation as a before/after comparison.
Refers to luck, fortune, and chance as though they are personal acquaintances with opinions.
Speaks much more clearly about what they don't want than about what they do.
Trails off when something is emotionally significant, filling the space with a gesture instead of words.
Uses a consistent metaphor for time — as a river, a ledger, a road — and extends it across long conversations.
Greets everyone by confirming something about their current state. 'You look like you've been traveling.' 'You slept well.'
States their disclaimers first, then their position, then their disclaimers again.
Switches between being extremely forthcoming and extremely guarded at seemingly random points in the conversation.
Offers alternatives to every proposal before accepting or declining. 'We could do that, or we could do this instead.'
Speaks about hypothetical scenarios as if they have already happened. 'If one were to do such a thing — and one did — then one would find...'
Uses physical comedy — timing, gesture, expression — to punctuate dry statements.
Describes people by what they remind them of rather than by their actual qualities.
Tends to find the exact wrong moment to be funny.
Reads mood from the room and mirrors it back, which makes them seem very agreeable until you realize they've said nothing.
References previous conversations in this location even if this is the first time the party has been here.
Gives very specific warnings without any context. 'Don't mention the eastern road.' 'Avoid the word formal.'
Speaks as though translating simultaneously from thought to language, with visible pauses during difficult passages.
Uses the names of local streets, buildings, and landmarks as shorthand for entire categories of thing.
Can't lie without telling you they're lying and then telling you a lie. They believe this counts as honesty.
Speaks about violence with the precise, neutral vocabulary of a professional who has handled a great deal of it.
Uses indirect speech for their own statements as a kind of diffusion of responsibility. 'There are those who feel...'
Never swears but chooses extremely specific non-swear replacements that are somehow worse.
Describes complex situations using elaborate fictional scenarios that are only thinly veiled.
Speaks about their own past self with the affection and mild condescension of someone describing a former employee.
Uses seasonal metaphors — 'a winter move,' 'a harvest decision,' 'spring thinking' — to describe the quality of ideas.
Routinely underestimates how much they know, which leads to them explaining things they then qualify as 'just my impression.'
Ends declarative statements with the rising intonation of a question, making certainty sound like doubt.
Responds to bad news with the phrase 'well, that's interesting' and means it.
Speaks as if they've already said everything before and is tired of explaining.
Uses first names only in moments of significant emphasis.
Begins every morning encounter by providing an unprompted weather report for the day.
Refers to the body's responses as independent entities. 'My hands decided to shake.' 'My voice had other plans.'
Talks about concepts they invented as if they are widely understood technical terms.
Has a characteristic phrase for buying time — 'let me think,' 'give me a moment,' 'just a second' — that appears before any important decision.
Makes strong, clear statements and then erases them immediately. 'This will work. Well. It should work. Likely.'
Frames other people's obvious interests as surprising discoveries. 'It turns out you care about money. Interesting.'
Uses extremely specific analogies from specialized fields that most listeners can't follow.
Speaks carefully and slowly about easy things, and quickly and carelessly about difficult ones.
Names the genre or type of thing before describing it. 'The kind of person who—' 'A situation like this one—' 'What usually happens when—'
Uses the phrase 'as you know' before information the listener definitely does not know.
Ends every substantive statement with a brief pause that invites disagreement. Continues only when it doesn't come.
Refers to professional knowledge as instinct and personal instinct as professional knowledge.
Identifies the center of any argument quickly and speaks directly to it, which can feel like interruption but isn't.
Describes people they dislike without mentioning dislike, using pure description that communicates everything.
Avoids the word 'but' entirely, replacing it with 'and also,' which changes the feel of every disagreement.
Uses specific brand names, trade names, or regional product names for generic items.
Speaks about probability and certainty as though they live on a dial they can read. 'I'd say sixty-forty.' 'Call it likely but not certain.'
Has a different voice — pitch, speed, register — for different types of information. Happy news fast, bad news slow.
Can't deliver a compliment without phrasing it as a neutral observation. 'You've done this before' instead of 'you're skilled.'
Uses narrative structures for all explanation. 'It begins with...' 'Eventually...' 'The conclusion was...'
Refers to everything they do reluctantly as something they 'ended up' doing rather than chose.
Can't accept 'thank you' — redirects immediately to what could have gone better.
Speaks in finished thoughts but pauses visibly at the boundary between them, creating a rhythm.
Uses diminishing scales for bad news. 'Not ideal. Not good. Not survivable. Let's say challenging.'
Describes everything they're looking forward to as though it has already happened and was fine.
Speaks in an extremely measured way that breaks down without warning into extraordinary animation, then resumes.
Never says the name of a topic they consider dangerous. Refers to it with consistent circumlocutions that everyone around them has learned.
Describes decisions they've made as though someone else made them. 'One decided.' 'It was determined.'
Speaks in a voice so neutral that intent is always ambiguous — never quite complimenting, never quite insulting.
Starts stories at the ending and works backward to explain how they got there.
Uses the future tense for hypotheticals. 'If you were to ask me, I will say...' 'Should you need it, there will be...'
Names the lesson before telling the story that illustrates it.
Uses exquisite politeness as an aggressive tool, without irony.
Inverts idiomatic expressions naturally, without humor, producing phrases that are technically comprehensible.
Speaks to groups as if addressing each person individually in sequence, which gives speeches an unusual intimacy.
Introduces any piece of information they're uncertain about by saying 'I believe' — and only then.
Uses geographic language to describe interpersonal dynamics. 'We're in difficult territory.' 'They've moved closer.'
Repeats requests with slight variations until they've been clearly acknowledged.
Can render a person's entire argument invalid with a single sentence and does so with regret.
Talks to animals, objects, and absent people in exactly the same register as present ones.
Uses silence the way others use 'yes.' Nods, waits, continues — all without verbal confirmation.
Has a specific verbal tic that emerges only when they're being deceptive — a repeated word, a phrase, a sound.
Talks about their own feelings only through what they notice others feeling. 'You seem frustrated' means they are.
Uses technical jargon from their trade constantly, without definition, and expects listeners to keep up.
States their conclusion last, after assembling every supporting piece in sequence — effective but long.
Gives warnings disguised as advice. 'One finds it helpful to avoid the east wing after dark.'
Uses extremely specific body language language. 'My hands are open. I'm turning toward you. Read that as you like.'
Speaks as though they're performing for an audience of people better informed than the one they're actually addressing.
Says exactly what they mean, every time, without social padding or softening. It reads as blunt but is simply precise.
